current_fact.fact_text: In 1741, a fire at New York’s Fort George, the governor’s residence, sparked panic that blossomed into the so‑called New York Conspiracy. Authorities claimed a multiracial plot of enslaved people and poor whites was behind a string of blazes, leading to trials and executions that today look more like mass hysteria than solved crime.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

current_fact.match_reason: A fevered conspiracy scare in colonial New York is matched with an episode about travel and perspective, inviting listeners to step back and reconsider how fear and rumors shape history.

queue.fact_text: In 1644, the Third Anglo‑Powhatan War erupted in colonial Virginia after a surprise attack by Powhatan forces on English settlements. It marked one of the last major attempts by Indigenous peoples in that region to push back against an encroaching colonial society reshaping their homelands.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: An Indigenous war against colonization in Virginia resonates with Tuareg music born from displacement, resistance, and the fight to keep ancestral land and culture alive.

queue.fact_text: On March 18, 1848, Milan erupted in the "Five Days of Milan," an urban uprising that drove Austrian marshal Radetzky and his troops temporarily from the city. Ordinary citizens turned streets and rooftops into battlegrounds, foreshadowing Italy’s long struggle for unification.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: A city‑wide uprising in Milan pairs with an episode focused on another city, Sheffield, where music becomes a way to tell urban stories of class, struggle, and everyday rebellion.

queue.fact_text: In 1793, during the Flanders Campaign of the French Revolutionary Wars, the Battle of Neerwinden saw French forces under Dumouriez defeated by an Austrian army. The loss shook revolutionary France and helped push Dumouriez toward an attempted defection, proof that ideals and personal ambition often collided on 18th‑century battlefields.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: A bloody field where power was contested contrasts with the modern 'battlefield' of the dancefloor, where women claim space and agency through music instead of muskets.

queue.fact_text: On this day in 1915’s global war, Adolf Hitler and Benito Mussolini had not yet risen, but by March 18, 1940, they met dramatically at the Brenner Pass in the Alps. There, surrounded by peaks and snow, they sealed their Axis partnership against France and Britain, turning a mountain crossing into a historical fault line.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A fateful Axis summit at an Alpine pass is paired with an episode shaped by Italian mountain landscapes, transforming a setting of wartime strategy into one of shared music and memory.

queue.fact_text: In 1241, Mongol forces swept into Poland and overwhelmed local armies at the Battle of Chmielnik, opening the way to plunder Kraków. For medieval Europeans, the riders from the east felt almost like an unstoppable force of nature, appearing suddenly and vanishing just as fast.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: A medieval invasion of Poland is balanced by a contemporary, peaceful hitchhiking journey through the same country, with modern Polish tracks rewriting the sonic memory of those landscapes.

queue.fact_text: On March 18, 1969, the United States began "Operation Menu," a secret bombing campaign against the Sihanouk Trail in Cambodia used by North Vietnamese and Viet Cong forces. The strikes were kept hidden not only from the public but even from much of the U.S. Congress, illustrating how modern wars can expand into the shadows.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A covert bombing campaign and concealed information connect with an episode about invisible infrastructures—phones, networks, and the unseen systems that shape how we experience reality.

queue.fact_text: In 1980, a Vostok‑2M rocket being fueled at Plesetsk Cosmodrome’s Site 43 exploded, killing 48 people in one of the deadliest accidents in the history of rocketry. The disaster was kept largely secret at the time, a tragic reminder that reaching space has often meant learning through catastrophe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A launchpad explosion and the dangerous energy of rockets align with an 'Electric Shock' episode full of high-voltage guitars and surging intensity.

queue.fact_text: On March 18, 1994, Bosniak and Croat leaders signed the Washington Agreement, ending their war against each other in Bosnia and creating a joint federation. In the middle of a brutal conflict, former enemies agreed to share institutions, showing how quickly battle lines can be redrawn when survival demands cooperation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A hard-won peace agreement and the slow work of rebuilding mirror an episode about learning, patience, and the gradual rewiring needed to move from conflict to collaboration.

queue.fact_text: In 37 CE, the Roman Senate annulled Emperor Tiberius’s will and declared Gaius Julius Caesar Augustus Germanicus—better known as Caligula, "Little Boots"—as emperor. For a brief moment he was adored as a bringer of change, before his rule descended into the infamy that still haunts his name.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

queue.match_reason: An emperor known by a nickname and a carefully constructed public persona fits an episode about alter egos and the multiple faces a single person (or ruler) can present.

queue.fact_text: On March 18, 1899-era science also looked upward from another angle: William Henry Pickering’s photographic work not only revealed Phoebe but pioneered a method—astrophotography—that would soon become standard. By trapping starlight on glass, astronomers could 'freeze time' and see faint objects the human eye alone could never catch.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Capturing distant celestial bodies on glass plates parallels capturing elusive orcas on camera and in memory—both are about patiently recording fleeting, remote wonders.

queue.fact_text: The 1848 premiere of *Leonora* in Philadelphia also marked an early moment when American musical identity began to diverge from Europe’s orbit. While critics were divided, the performance showed that large, ambitious works could be composed in—and for—the young United States, not just imported from abroad.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: An American attempt at grand opera pairs with an episode about Flamenco’s evolution—both trace how local traditions take on large, theatrical forms and claim space on the world’s stages.

queue.fact_text: The 1899 discovery of Phoebe using photographs was the first time a planetary moon was found via a technological 'memory' rather than a human’s live gaze. Astronomers could return to those plates again and again, extracting new information long after the original observation night had passed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

queue.match_reason: Streets of London captured in memory and music mirror how photographic plates captured Saturn’s faint companions—both are snapshots you can revisit, noticing new details with each return.

queue.fact_text: By repealing the Stamp Act in 1766, Britain tried to dial down a crisis without realizing it had helped teach colonists the power of coordinated resistance. Pamphlets, newspapers, and public debate—all the printed matter once taxed—would soon become tools for spreading revolutionary ideas even faster.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: The birth of American resistance through pamphlets and protests fits an episode devoted to Residente, an artist who turns words and rhythm into modern tools of political pressure.

published.fact_text: In 1965, Alexei Leonov opened the hatch of Voskhod 2 and became the first human to walk in space, floating outside his spacecraft for 12 minutes. His suit ballooned so much in the vacuum that he had to bleed off pressure and squeeze back through the hatch headfirst, improvising his way back to safety.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal first step into outer space pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space and shifting perspective.

published.fact_text: In 1899, astronomers identified Phoebe, a distant moon of Saturn, using photographic plates taken months earlier. Unlike Saturn’s bright inner moons, Phoebe orbits backward, is unusually dark, and may be a captured Kuiper Belt object, making it a cosmic interloper in Saturn’s sky.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: A strange, retrograde moon discovered on old plates fits the futuristic, space-tinted pulse of a 'Cosmic Monday' dance selection.

published.fact_text: On March 18, 1848, William Henry Fry’s opera *Leonora* premiered in Philadelphia, becoming the first known grand opera by an American composer to be publicly performed. It was an ambitious attempt to prove that large-scale, European-style opera could grow roots in the New World.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: A bold 'first' in American opera history resonates with Sedna’s own inaugural episode, both marking the launch of something ambitiously new in sound.

published.fact_text: On this day in 1959, the Hawaii Admission Act was signed into law, clearing the way for the islands to become the 50th U.S. state. It transformed a remote Pacific archipelago into a symbol of both postwar American expansion and the blending of Indigenous, Asian, and Western cultures.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: Hawaii’s statehood, wrapped in sunlit Pacific imagery, pairs with an episode built around coastlines, long summers, and warm, flowing jazz.

published.fact_text: In 1944, Mount Vesuvius erupted in the middle of World War II, sending ash over nearby towns and destroying dozens of Allied bombers parked at an airfield. It was a rare moment when a volcano, rather than an enemy army, grounded air power in a global war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'é Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: An eruption from Italy’s most iconic volcano fits an episode explicitly themed around Italy’s "volcanic pulse" and fiery southern energy.

published.fact_text: On March 18, 1953, a powerful earthquake struck western Turkey, devastating towns around Yenice and Gönen and killing over a thousand people. The event highlighted how the North Anatolian Fault, one of Earth’s major fracture lines, runs beneath communities that must live with a restless crust.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: After the violence of an earthquake, the idea of a 'Slow Planet' offers a sonic counterweight: calming, grounding downtempo to reflect on Earth’s power and fragility.

published.fact_text: A thousand years earlier, in 1068, a series of massive earthquakes shook the Levant and Arabian Peninsula, reportedly killing up to 20,000 people. Medieval chroniclers described cities shattered and landscapes reshaped, long before plate tectonics offered any explanation for such upheaval.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: An ancient Near Eastern catastrophe connects with a trip-hop journey that includes 'Lebanese Blonde', bridging geography and a moody, cinematic sense of shifting ground.

published.fact_text: In 1971, a huge landslide thundered into Yanawayin Lake in Peru, generating a wave that swept away the Chungar mining camp and killing around 200 people. It was a reminder that mountains can fail catastrophically, and that water can become a weapon when rock meets lake at speed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A devastating wave born from a mountainside fits with an episode obsessed with water, depth, and immersion, offering a gentler, reflective counter-story about our relationship to lakes and seas.

published.fact_text: On March 18, 1915, during the Gallipoli campaign of World War I, three Allied battleships were sunk in a single day while trying to force the Dardanelles. The strait, a narrow seam between Europe and Asia, became a graveyard of steel and an early lesson in how geography can outmatch firepower.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A naval disaster on the sea route to the Black Sea is matched with an episode lived entirely on a boat, shifting from warships in peril to peaceful, human-scale voyages over water.

published.fact_text: In 1766, the British Parliament repealed the Stamp Act, a tax on printed materials in the American colonies that had triggered boycotts, riots, and the slogan 'no taxation without representation.' The repeal was a tactical retreat that, ironically, convinced many colonists that resistance could work.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A tax revolt and its political impact fit perfectly with an episode centered on Rage Against the Machine, whose music is steeped in protest against power and economic injustice.

published.fact_text: In 1741, a fire at New York’s Fort George, the governor’s residence, sparked panic that blossomed into the so‑called New York Conspiracy. Authorities claimed a multiracial plot of enslaved people and poor whites was behind a string of blazes, leading to trials and executions that today look more like mass hysteria than solved crime.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A fevered conspiracy scare in colonial New York is matched with an episode about travel and perspective, inviting listeners to step back and reconsider how fear and rumors shape history.
